summary: O'Neil critiques a panel of male tech executives at the Grace Hopper Celebration whose advice for women was predictably unhelpful ('lean in,' 'speak up'). She argues that being a true male ally requires two fundamental steps: first, actually believing women when they report experiencing sexism, and second, actively intervening when witnessing sexist behavior rather than leaving women to handle it alone. She shares her frustration that even men close to her have dismissed her own experiences of sexism, illustrating how difficult the first step proves to be.
key_points: Corporate male allies at tech conferences offer hollow, predictable advice that places the burden on women to change their behavior
The most basic requirement for male allyship is simply believing women when they describe experiencing sexism
Even well-meaning men often fail this basic test, dismissing women's reported experiences
True allyship requires men to actively intervene when they witness sexism, not leave women to confront it alone
Acknowledging sexism requires empathy and will lead to disappointment in the world, but that's the reality
money_quotes: The advice was stale and trite and included things like 'speak up,' 'lean in,' and 'get excited about your ideas like men do.'
Trust women who say they experience sexism.
When I repeated this to a couple of them, they actually said that I didn't experience the stuff that I had.
Yes, it requires empathy, and observation, and yes it sucks, because once you start seeing it you will be disappointed in the world. Tough shit, it's reality.
I don't know how many times I've been the target of sexism in front of other men and somehow it's my job to confront it and deal with it.
Men, step the fuck up and, when you see sexism happening, once you can manage that, defend the target and put a stop to it.
key_insight: Male allyship begins with the deceptively simple act of believing women's experiences of sexism, then progresses to actively intervening rather than expecting women to fight these battles alone.
